title: Higher Profit, Lower Risk? How Large Language Models Shape Decision Behavior in Supply Chain Optimization

agents.description: Commander agent that receives user queries, delegates tasks to the Writer, coordinates the iterative solution-generation loop, executes generated code, logs results, and relays final answers to the user.

Writer agent that receives the user question and problem context (role, problem background, optimization model code, in-context learning examples, predefined helper functions) from the Commander, generates executable code to solve the query, and compiles the final natural language answer based on execution results.
Safeguard agent that verifies safety and compliance of the code generated by the Writer before execution. Note: disabled during the experiment to reduce latency.

orchestration.orchestration_pattern_description: A structured multi-agent framework (adapted from OptiGuide by Li et al. 2023) where a Commander orchestrates a Writer and Safeguard in an iterative loop. The Commander receives user queries, delegates to the Writer for code generation, routes code to the Safeguard for validation, executes cleared code, and returns results to the Writer for final answer compilation before relaying to the user. The loop repeats until the question is answered or a timeout/maximum number of tries is reached.

multi_agent_coordination_strategy.multi_agent_coordination_strategy_descri: Three cooperative agents (Commander, Writer, Safeguard) with distinct roles collaborate in a centralized, iterative pipeline. The Commander acts as the central coordinator, delegating subtasks to the Writer and Safeguard sequentially, aggregating results, and managing the overall interaction flow.

human_integration.human_integration_description: Participants interact with the LLM-optimization decision support system via a natural language chat interface embedded in a behavioral experiment platform (oTree). Humans initiate queries freely, receive answers generated by the multi-agent framework, and make final supply chain activation decisions independently. The system provides decision support but does not enforce any particular choice. Human decisions are logged and compared against elicited risk preferences.

retrieval.application_domain_description: Stochastic supply chain network design involving selection of suppliers and production/roasting sites under disruption risk, balancing expected profit against risk exposure.
retrieval.problem_description: Major supply chain software vendors are integrating LLM-based capabilities into their platforms, but it is unclear whether enabling dynamic, conversational interactions with embedded optimization improves supply chain decisions. Specifically, it is unknown whether LLM-aided optimization interfaces guide individuals toward normatively superior decisions and whether they facilitate alignment between decisions and individual risk-reward preferences.
retrieval.proposed_solution: An interactive LLM-based decision support framework integrating GPT-4o with a mathematical optimization solver (Gurobi) via a multi-agent architecture (Commander, Writer, Safeguard) adapted from OptiGuide. The system allows users to interact via natural language chat to explore supply chain configurations, run stochastic optimization, and receive explanations. A behavioral lab experiment with 106 participants compares a treatment group (with chat access) against a control group (without chat) on a stochastic supply chain network design task, and elicits individual risk preferences via a lottery-style choice task to assess decision alignment.
retrieval.research_methodology_description: An incentivized, controlled behavioral lab experiment conducted at the experimenTUM lab (TU Munich) with 106 participants randomly assigned to treatment (with LLM-optimization chat) or control (without chat) groups. Part 1 involves a supply chain design task under uncertainty; Part 2 elicits risk preferences via a lottery-style choice task. Statistical analyses (proportion z-tests, logistic regressions, Mann-Whitney U tests) compare decision quality and preference alignment across groups.

decision_quality_improvement: Participants with chat access selected non-dominated solutions in 60% of cases vs. 43% without chat access (p=0.040, two-proportion z-test).
Chat-enabled participants were more likely to select the expected profit-maximizing solution (p=0.046 for question-asking behavior in logistic regression).
Under a relaxed 2.5% frontier threshold, non-dominated selection rates were 70% (With Chat) vs. 53% (Without Chat), p=0.036.
Participants with chat access showed closer alignment between Part 1 decisions and problem-specific risk preferences elicited in Part 2.
Participants with chat access spent more time on the decision task (6.0 min vs. 4.1 min, p=0.005), reflecting greater engagement.
response_time_impact: The Safeguard agent was disabled during the experiment to reduce latency and ensure a smoother user experience.
System response times were optimized during the pilot study based on participant feedback.

ce_technique_effectiveness.in-context-learning: Extended in-context learning examples were added during pilot to expand the framework's answering capabilities, improving response quality for diverse participant queries.
ce_technique_effectiveness.iterative-reasoning: The iterative Commander-Writer-Safeguard loop enabled dynamic code generation and execution to answer complex optimization queries, supporting multi-turn conversations.

failure_modes: Participants with chat access frequently overshot their risk preferences, taking on more risk than intended due to profit-focused queries.
Chat interactions driven by profit-oriented or short/ambiguous questions led to decisions misaligned with participants' underlying risk preferences.
General (domain-agnostic) risk preferences had no impact on decision-making in the With Chat group, indicating that chat content overrides individual risk attitudes.
48 out of 163 questions involved imprecise wording (missing roastery capacity levels, or asking exclusively about suppliers or roasteries), potentially leading to suboptimal system responses.
System limitations such as response latency from external API may moderate participant engagement compared to enterprise-grade solutions.
limitations: Moderate sample size (106 participants); replication with larger and more diverse populations needed for stronger external validity.
Technically advanced but constrained LLM-optimization interface; system limitations (e.g., API response latency) may moderate engagement.
All participants received the profit-maximizing benchmark solution, likely understating treatment effects; a more neutral reference point might widen the performance gap.
Explicit risk cues in instructions may have partially mitigated overshooting; without them, overshooting could be more pronounced.
Individual behavioral channels (e.g., awareness of alternatives, trust, comprehension) cannot be fully isolated due to the realistic, composite experimental design.
Experiment conducted in English, which may affect generalizability across language groups.
Results should be interpreted as evidence that conversational optimization interfaces influence decision processes, not as proof that all chat framework elements drive superior outcomes equally.

baseline_methods: Control group without chat access (no LLM-based decision support)
Static risk-neutral benchmark solution maximizing expected profit (provided to all participants)
identified_research_gaps: Replication with larger and more diverse populations to strengthen external validity and enable more granular investigations of behavioral heterogeneity

Isolating specific behavioral drivers (e.g., knowledge of alternative solutions, trust in the interface, comprehension and situational awareness of risk-reward preferences) to refine theory on human-algorithm co-creation
Exploring how LLM-powered optimization interfaces influence cooperation and coordination in settings involving multiple human decision-makers
Designing interfaces to support effective collaborative decisions in multi-human settings
Developing a more general theory of human-algorithm co-creation of advice for complex supply chain decision-making
Investigating the effect of more neutral reference points (e.g., previous manager's decision) instead of the profit-maximizing benchmark
Future research on interface design, user behavior, and LLM-augmented decision support in various decision contexts
